does: This paper introduces PSRD, a phase-wise self-reward decoding framework that mitigates vision hallucination in large vision-language models at inference time without requiring external supervision. The core contribution is identifying that visual hallucination exhibits dynamic patterns that peak at the onset of semantic phases during generation. The method uses phase-wise self-reward signals to detect and correct hallucinations in real-time, and distills the guidance signal into a lightweight reward model that efficiently provides on-the-fly intervention during decoding. This approach eliminates the need for large-scale annotated data for fine-tuning and moves beyond static post-hoc correction strategies.
connects: This work builds on recent research addressing hallucination in vision-language models, extending beyond earlier post-hoc filtering and correction methods that treat hallucination as a static problem. It connects to the broader literature on self-supervised and self-rewarding approaches in language models, drawing from frameworks where models generate their own training signals. The paper also engages with reward modeling and decoding-time intervention techniques developed in areas like reinforcement learning from human feedback and controlled generation. Additionally, it relates to prior work analyzing failure modes in multimodal systems and the dynamics of generation errors, extending these insights from temporal patterns in purely text-based models to the multimodal setting.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more efficient hallucination mitigation in vision-language models without costly annotation or retraining cycles. The discovery of phase-wise hallucination dynamics could inform future model architecture designs and training procedures that anticipate and prevent hallucination emergence at semantic boundaries. The lightweight reward model distillation approach enables deployment of hallucination-aware decoding on resource-constrained systems. More broadly, this work suggests techniques for detecting and intervening on dynamic failure modes during inference across multimodal tasks. Future research could extend phase-wise analysis to other failure modes in language models, develop more sophisticated reward distillation methods, or investigate whether similar temporal patterns appear in other vision-language phenomena.
